content:
# AGENTS.md — Lumen GM Hub

> Operational guidelines for Claude Code and Codex when working on Lumen.
> Read `plan.md` first for project context and current milestone.
> Last updated: 2026-03-13

## Project Identity

- **Name:** Lumen GM Hub
- **Type:** Desktop application (Electron) + Foundry VTT module + browser-based voice client
- **Domain:** TTRPG tooling — PF2e games on Foundry VTT v13
- **Current milestone:** v0.2 — Suggestion engine + settings + triage UI
- **Owner:** Cole

## Golden Rules

1. **v0.2 scope only.** Build the suggestion engine, settings panel, and suggestion queue UI. Do not build campaign memory (v0.3), the Campaign Setup Wizard (v0.4), post-session mode (v0.5), or write permissions (v0.6). If a feature isn't in the v0.2 step list in `plan.md`, it doesn't get built.
2. **Clean and usable, not gold-plated.** The Hub UI should be visually clean and comfortable for a 3–4 hour session. It does NOT need to be pixel-perfect or use advanced animations. Invest in readability and usability, not polish for its own sake.
3. **Architecture decisions require ADRs.** Any significant technical choice (frameworks, project structure, data formats, protocol design) gets documented as an ADR in `brain/Lumen/lumen-architecture.md` with context, options, rationale, and tradeoffs. This is how future-Cole (and future-Code) will understand why things are the way they are.
4. **Two-AI review loop.** Changes to this file (`AGENTS.md`) or `plan.md` require GPT-4 review before applying. Flag this in session if Cole asks for changes.
5. **Brain files are the memory system.** Decisions, facts, and open questions go in `brain/Lumen/*.md` files. Journal entries (`journal/Lumen/YYYY-MM-DD.md`) are append-only session logs — not the same location. Do NOT write journals to `brain/journal/`.
6. **PoC code is reference, not foundation (unless Step 0 decides otherwise).** The first task is evaluating PoC code quality and deciding what carries forward. Until that decision is made, treat `poc/` as read-only reference material.

## Tech Stack & Environment

### Core Stack (Validated in PoC)
| Component | Technology | Status |
|---|---|---|
| Transcription | `faster-whisper` 1.1.1 (CTranslate2, float16) | Validated — carry forward |
| VAD | Silero VAD (threshold 0.65, min 300ms, RMS gate 0.01) | Validated — carry forward |
| Whisper model | `large-v3` default | Validated — ADR-006 |
| Architecture | Single shared Whisper instance + segment queue | Validated — ADR-007 |
| VoIP (GM) | Electron + WebRTC | Validated — carry forward |
| VoIP (Player) | Browser HTML/JS + WebRTC | Validated — carry forward |
| Signaling | WebSocket server (localhost for v0.1) | Validated — carry forward |
| Audio codec | Opus (48kHz, 64kbps) | Validated |

### New for v0.1 (Requires Evaluation)
| Component | Decision Needed | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Hub UI framework | Evaluate in Step 0c | React, Svelte, vanilla, or other. Must support clean, usable UI within Electron |
| Foundry module language | Evaluate in Step 0b | JavaScript (Foundry standard) or TypeScript. Consider ecosystem norms |
| Project structure | Evaluate in Step 0a | Restructure PoC modules or clean rewrite |
| Monorepo tooling | Decide during Step 1 | pnpm workspaces, Turborepo, or flat structure. Consider that the project has Python + Node.js + Foundry module |

### Hardware Reference
- **Development GPU:** NVIDIA RTX 3070 (8GB VRAM), CUDA 13.2, Driver 595.79
- **Pipeline VRAM:** ~6,349 MB avg under 3-stream load with `large-v3`
- **Practical VRAM ceiling:** ~6.5 GB (leave headroom for Foundry + browser)
- **Python:** 3.12.10 (venv at `C:\Dev\Lumen\.venv`)
- **CUDA Toolkit:** 13.2

### Not In Stack (Intentionally — Unchanged from PoC)
| Excluded | Why |
|---|---|
| pyannote.audio | Per-stream VoIP solves speaker attribution — ADR-001 |
| VB-Cable / virtual audio | VoIP replaces loopback — ADR-001 |
| WASAPI | No loopback needed — ADR-001 |
| Discord SDK/API | Lumen provides its own voice — ADR-002 |

## v0.1 Step-Specific Guidelines

### Step 0 — Architecture Decisions (Day 1)

This is the most important step. Every decision made here cascades through the rest of v0.1.

**0a. PoC code assessment:**
- Read every file in `poc/shared/`, `poc/phase2/hub/`, `poc/phase2/signaling/`, `poc/phase2/player-client/`, and `poc/phase3/`
- Assess: type safety, error handling, separation of concerns, dependency management, test coverage
- Produce a written assessment with a clear recommendation: restructure or rewrite
- If restructuring: identify exactly which modules carry forward and what changes
- If rewriting: identify what PoC patterns to preserve and what to improve
- Document as an ADR

**0b. Foundry module language:**
- Review Foundry v13 module development docs
- Check what the PF2e system module uses
- Consider: Cole's TypeScript experience (limited), Foundry ecosystem norms, build tooling overhead
- Document as an ADR

**0c. Hub UI framework:**
- The UI must be clean and usable for multi-hour GM sessions
- Consider: Electron integration, bundle size, learning curve for Cole, component ecosystem
- The PoC used raw HTML — that's not sufficient for v0.1
- Document as an ADR

**All three decisions must be written and confirmed by Cole before Step 1 begins.**

### Step 1 — Project Restructure

- Set up the production project structure based on Step 0 decisions
- If restructuring PoC code: copy validated modules to `src/`, refactor for production quality
- If rewriting: scaffold new modules, referencing PoC implementations
- Archive all PoC code to `poc/` — don't delete it
- Validate that the core pipeline still works from the new structure (mic → VAD → Whisper → terminal)
- Set up proper `.gitignore`, `README.md`, and dependency management
- Git commit convention changes from `phase1:` / `phase2:` to `v0.1:` prefix

### Step 2 — Foundry Module

- The module connects to the Hub, NOT the other way around. Hub is the server, Foundry module is the client.
- WebSocket connection on `ws://127.0.0.1:17172` (spec Section 20)
- Pairing flow: one-time code exchange, token persisted in Foundry world settings
- Module sends game state; Hub receives it. **Read-only in v0.1.** No write-back.
- Game state to stream: active scene, combat state (encounter, initiative, current turn), party roster (actors, HP, conditions)
- Send full state on initial connection, then deltas on change
- The module must survive: scene changes, combat start/stop, player join/leave, Foundry reload
- Test against Foundry v13 with the PF2e system module loaded
- **Do not build:** write permissions, Campaign Ledger, compendium queries, player-facing features

### Step 3 — Hub Transcript Viewer UI

- Dark theme, high contrast, readable at arm's length on a secondary monitor
- Layout: Connection/Foundry status bar at top, main transcript panel in center, players sidebar, session controls at bottom
- Transcript: speaker names color-coded, timestamps, auto-scroll with scroll-lock on manual scroll-up
- Foundry state: scene name, combat round/turn — compact display, not a full Foundry mirror
- Session controls: start/stop, timer, share link button
- Responsive to different window sizes (full screen on secondary monitor OR half-screen alongside Foundry)
- **Keyboard accessible:** Tab navigation, hotkeys for session start/stop
- **Do not build:** suggestion queue, conflict alerts, memory browser, settings panel, post-session report

### Step 4 — Integration Testing

- Reuse the Phase 3 stability harness concept for the simulated multi-player test
- Test with Foundry VTT running, PF2e system loaded, active scene + mock combat
- Session log format: JSONL with transcript segments + Foundry context events
- Verify no regressions from PoC benchmarks (VRAM, latency, stability)
- Fix any integration bugs before declaring v0.1 complete

## Coding Standards

### Python (Pipeline)
- Python 3.12+ with type hints on all function signatures
- `asyncio` for concurrent stream processing
- Format with `ruff`
- Proper error handling: catch specific exceptions, log with context, re-raise if unrecoverable. No bare `except:` clauses. No silent catches.
- Dependencies pinned in `requirements.txt`
- Docstrings on public functions (one-line summary + args/returns for non-obvious functions)

### JavaScript/TypeScript (Hub + Foundry Module)
- TypeScript for the Hub (Electron) unless Step 0c decides otherwise
- Foundry module language per Step 0b decision
- Player browser client stays vanilla JS — single HTML file, zero dependencies
- Electron: `contextBridge` and `preload` for IPC. No `nodeIntegration: true`. No `remote`.
- ESLint for linting

### General
- Comments explain *why*, not *what*
- No premature abstraction — but v0.1 code should be structured for future extension (unlike PoC which was intentionally flat)
- Error handling: log clearly and recover gracefully where possible. A transcription error shouldn't crash the Hub.
- Git: commit at meaningful checkpoints. Messages follow `v0.1: description` convention.
- No console.log debugging left in committed code — use a proper logger

## What NOT To Do (v0.2)

- ❌ Don't build campaign memory or conflict detection (v0.3)
- ❌ Don't build the Campaign Setup Wizard or note import (v0.4)
- ❌ Don't build post-session mode (v0.5)
- ❌ Don't build write permissions from Hub → Foundry (v0.6)
- ❌ Don't build subscription/licensing/auth (v1.0)
- ❌ Don't build cloud tier infrastructure (v1.5+)
- ❌ Don't build TURN relay
- ❌ Don't add pyannote, VB-Cable, or any eliminated dependency
- ❌ Don't write journal entries to `brain/journal/` — journals go to `journal/Lumen/`

## Vault File Paths

These are the correct paths. Follow them exactly.

| Content | Path |
|---|---|
| Operational plan | `projects/Lumen/plan.md` |
| Codex guidelines | `projects/Lumen/AGENTS.md` |
| Full spec | `projects/Lumen/spec_v3.pdf` |
| Benchmark data | `projects/Lumen/benchmarks/*.md` |
| Project knowledge base | `brain/Lumen/lumen.md` |
| Architecture decisions | `brain/Lumen/lumen-architecture.md` |
| Business model | `brain/Lumen/lumen-business.md` |
| Session digests | `journal/Lumen/YYYY-MM-DD.md` |
| PoC source (archive) | `C:\Dev\Lumen\poc\` |
| v0.1 source | `C:\Dev\Lumen\src\` |

## Escalation Rules

- If a Step 0 decision can't be made confidently, **ask Cole.** Don't guess on foundational architecture.
- If the Foundry module can't connect to the Hub via WebSocket, debug the protocol before building more features on top of a broken connection.
- If the UI framework choice is causing friction mid-Step 3, flag it early — switching frameworks after 50% completion is expensive.
- If VRAM usage regresses from PoC benchmarks by > 10%, flag immediately.
- If a dependency requires network access beyond npm/pip installs, confirm with Cole.
- If a task would take more than ~2 hours of AI compute, break it into smaller chunks and confirm the approach first.

---
*This file governs AI behavior on the Lumen project. Changes require the two-AI review loop.*

# Lumen GM Hub — plan.md
> Last updated: 2026-03-12
> Full spec: `projects/lumen/spec_v3.pdf` (18 pages — reference doc, not for daily reading)

## What Is Lumen?

AI-powered companion app for PF2e games run in Foundry VTT (v13). Captures session audio via a **built-in VoIP client**, transcribes in real time with speaker attribution, generates GM-facing suggestions, maintains campaign memory, detects continuity conflicts, and writes session notes to Foundry.

## Current Phase: v0.1 — Pipeline + Foundry Connector + Transcript Viewer

**PoC completed 2026-03-11 with Go verdict.** The audio pipeline is validated. We are now building the first product milestone.

**v0.1 definition of "done":** A GM can start a session in Lumen, have players join via browser, see a live speaker-attributed transcript in the Hub, and see Foundry game state (scene, party, combat) reflected in the Hub UI. The transcript viewer is clean and usable — not a debug tool.

---

## Architecture (VoIP-First — Validated)

```
Player A ──WebRTC──→                              ──→ VAD → Whisper → [Player A]: "text"
Player B ──WebRTC──→   GM's Machine (Lumen Hub)   ──→ VAD → Whisper → [Player B]: "text"
Player C ──WebRTC──→                              ──→ VAD → Whisper → [Player C]: "text"
GM Mic   ──local───→                              ──→ VAD → Whisper → [GM]: "text"
                                                            │
                                                  Chronological merge → Attributed transcript
                                                            │
                              Foundry VTT ◄──WebSocket──► Lumen Hub (Electron)
                              (scene, combat,                │
                               party, actors)          Transcript Viewer UI
```

**Key architectural decisions (see `brain/Lumen/lumen-architecture.md` for full ADRs):**

- **VoIP-first, not Discord loopback.** Per-stream capture = deterministic speaker attribution. (ADR-001)
- **No pyannote, no VB-Cable, no WASAPI.** Eliminated by VoIP architecture. (ADR-001, ADR-002)
- **Electron for the Hub.** Bundles Chromium's WebRTC stack. (ADR-003)
- **`large-v3` Whisper model as default.** Confirmed on 8GB cards alongside Foundry. (ADR-006)
- **Single shared Whisper instance with segment queue.** CTranslate2 shares weights. (ADR-007)
- **Pipeline stability validated.** 60 min, 16,548 segments, 0 errors, no VRAM leak. (ADR-008)

## Tech Stack

| Component | Technology | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Transcription | `faster-whisper` (CTranslate2) | Local GPU, Python, `large-v3` default |
| Voice Activity Detection | Silero VAD | Per-stream, tuned (threshold 0.65, min 300ms, RMS gate 0.01) |
| VoIP (GM side) | Electron + WebRTC | Built into Hub |
| VoIP (Player side) | Browser HTML/JS + WebRTC | No install |
| Signaling | WebSocket server | Localhost for v0.1, cloud for production |
| Audio codec | Opus (48kHz, 64kbps default) | Configurable bitrate |
| Hub application | Electron | Desktop app |
| Foundry module | JavaScript or TypeScript | Evaluate as first task (see Step 0) |
| Hub UI framework | TBD | Evaluate as first task — must support clean, usable UI |

## VRAM Budget (RTX 3070 — 8GB, Validated)

| Whisper Model | VRAM Peak | Status |
|---|---|---|
| `large-v3` | ~5,851 MB (single), ~6,349 MB (3-stream sustained) | **Recommended default** |
| `medium` | ~3,899 MB | Fallback for constrained systems |

Total pipeline under real workload (Foundry + Hub + 3 streams): ~6.3 GB avg, ~2.3 GB headroom on 8GB card.

---

## v0.1 Build Plan

### Step 0 — Architecture Decisions & Code Strategy (Day 1)

Before writing any v0.1 code, evaluate and decide:

**0a. PoC code quality assessment.** Review the existing PoC codebase (`poc/shared/`, `poc/phase2/`, `poc/phase3/`). Decide whether to restructure the validated modules into a clean `src/` layout or do a clean rewrite referencing the PoC. Document the decision and rationale.

Factors to consider:
- Are the core modules (whisper_engine.py, vad.py, config.py) production-ready or PoC-quality?
- Is the Electron scaffolding from Phase 2 reusable or too hacky?
- Is the WebRTC signaling server worth carrying forward?
- How much technical debt exists in the PoC code?

**0b. Foundry module language.** Evaluate JavaScript vs TypeScript for the Foundry v13 module. Standard Foundry modules are JS, but TS offers type safety. Consider what the Foundry module ecosystem prefers and what will cause the least friction.

**0c. Hub UI framework.** The transcript viewer needs to be clean and usable — not a debug tool. Evaluate what UI approach works best within Electron (React, vanilla HTML/CSS, Svelte, etc.). The PoC used inline HTML — that won't scale for a real UI. Consider what Cole can maintain and iterate on.

**Output:** Written decisions for all three questions, added as ADRs to `brain/Lumen/lumen-architecture.md`. Project structure scaffolded based on decisions.

---

### Step 1 — Project Restructure (2–3 days)

Based on Step 0 decisions, set up the production project structure. Example layout (adapt based on decisions):

```
lumen/
├── src/
│   ├── pipeline/              ← audio pipeline (Python)
│   │   ├── whisper_engine.py  ← faster-whisper wrapper
│   │   ├── vad.py             ← Silero VAD wrapper
│   │   ├── transcription_server.py  ← WebSocket server receiving audio, producing transcript
│   │   ├── config.py          ← model config, VAD params
│   │   └── requirements.txt
│   ├── hub/                   ← Electron application
│   │   ├── main/              ← Electron main process
│   │   ├── renderer/          ← UI (framework TBD)
│   │   ├── preload/           ← contextBridge IPC
│   │   └── package.json
│   ├── signaling/             ← WebSocket signaling server
│   │   └── server.py (or .js)
│   ├── player-client/         ← browser voice client
│   │   └── index.html         ← single-file, zero-dependency
│   └── foundry-module/        ← Foundry VTT v13 module
│       ├── module.json        ← Foundry module manifest
│       ├── scripts/           ← module JS/TS
│       └── templates/         ← Handlebars templates (if needed)
├── poc/                       ← archived PoC code (reference only)
├── benchmarks/                ← carried forward from PoC
├── docs/
│   └── spec_v3.pdf
├── .gitignore
├── README.md
└── package.json (or monorepo config)
```

Acceptance criteria:
- Clean separation between pipeline (Python), Hub (Electron/JS), Foundry module, and player client
- PoC code archived in `poc/` — not deleted, but not imported into `src/`
- Core pipeline modules validated: can load Whisper, capture mic audio, transcribe, and output to terminal from the new structure
- All dependencies documented and pinned

---

### Step 2 — Foundry VTT Module: Read-Only Connector (1–2 weeks)

Build the Foundry v13 module that connects to the Lumen Hub.

**What the module does (v0.1):**
- Registers as a Foundry v13 module with a "Lumen Hub" panel in the sidebar
- Connects to the Hub via WebSocket on `ws://127.0.0.1:17172` (per spec Section 20)
- Pairing flow: Hub displays a pairing code, GM enters it in Foundry panel once, token stored in world settings
- Streams game state to the Hub on change:
  - Active scene (name, ID, thumbnail)
  - Combat state (active encounter, initiative order, current turn)
  - Party roster (actor names, player names, HP, conditions)
  - Actor updates (HP changes, condition add/remove during combat)
- Receives live transcript from Hub and displays in a read-only panel within Foundry (optional — may defer to v0.2)

**What the module does NOT do (v0.1):**
- No write-back to Foundry (no journal writes, no HP/condition changes from Hub)
- No Campaign Ledger creation
- No player-facing read-only snapshot
- No compendium queries (that's for the suggestion engine in v0.2)

**Key references:**
- Spec Section 3.4 (Foundry module component)
- Spec Section 5.1 (pairing flow)
- Spec Section 20 (WebSocket API contract)

Acceptance criteria:
- Module installs in Foundry v13 via manifest URL or local folder
- Successful WebSocket connection to Hub with pairing flow
- Hub receives scene, combat, and party data in real time as GM navigates Foundry
- Connection survives scene changes and combat start/stop without dropping

---

### Step 3 — Hub Transcript Viewer UI (1–2 weeks)

Rebuild the Electron Hub UI from the PoC debug page into a clean, usable interface.

**Panels (visible simultaneously during session):**

1. **Connection Status** — signaling server status, Foundry pairing status, VoIP session state
2. **Connected Players** — list of connected players with speaking indicators and connection quality
3. **Foundry State** — active scene name, combat status (round/turn), party roster summary. Proves the Foundry module is feeding data to the Hub
4. **Live Transcript** — scrolling real-time transcript with:
   - Speaker name (color-coded per player)
   - Timestamp
   - Transcription text
   - Latency indicator (subtle, for debugging — not prominent)
   - Auto-scroll with manual scroll lock (if GM scrolls up, don't snap back)
5. **Session Controls** — Start/Stop session, session duration timer, session link for sharing with players

**Design principles:**
- Dark theme (GMs play in dim rooms)
- High contrast text for readability during sessions
- Compact enough for a secondary monitor or a side panel alongside Foundry
- No visual clutter — every element earns its screen space
- Keyboard accessible (tab between panels, hotkeys for session start/stop)

**What the UI does NOT include (v0.1):**
- No suggestion queue or triage actions (v0.2)
- No conflict alerts (v0.3)
- No campaign memory browser (v0.3)
- No post-session report (v0.5)
- No settings/preferences panel beyond basic session controls

Acceptance criteria:
- GM can start a session, see players connect, see Foundry state update, and read a live transcript — all from one window
- UI is visually clean and usable for a 3–4 hour session without eye strain
- Transcript auto-scrolls during active play but doesn't fight the GM if they scroll up to re-read something
- Works on both primary and secondary monitors (responsive to different window sizes)

---

### Step 4 — Integration & Session Testing (1 week)

Wire everything together and run a real integrated test.

**Integration targets:**
- VoIP voice connection (Hub + browser player clients)
- Per-stream transcription pipeline (VAD → Whisper → labeled transcript)
- Foundry module streaming game state to Hub
- Hub UI displaying transcript + Foundry state simultaneously
- Session logging to local file (JSONL + readable text)

**Test scenarios:**
- Solo test: GM + 1 browser player tab, Foundry running with an active scene and mock combat
- Simulated multi-player: pipe test audio through multiple streams (reuse Phase 3 stability harness)
- Scene change: switch scenes in Foundry, verify Hub UI updates
- Combat: start encounter in Foundry, advance initiative, verify Hub reflects combat state
- Session lifecycle: start session → play for 15+ minutes → stop session → verify log file written

**Stability validation:**
- Run the simulated multi-player test for 30+ minutes
- Monitor VRAM and latency (reuse Phase 3 monitoring approach)
- Confirm no regressions from PoC benchmarks

Acceptance criteria:
- All integration targets working in a single session
- No crashes or degradation over 30+ minutes
- Session log file contains clean, speaker-attributed transcript with Foundry context events (scene changes, combat rounds)
- v0.1 milestone is complete

---

## v0.1 Summary

| Step | Work | Duration | Dependencies |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | Architecture decisions + code strategy | 1 day | None |
| 1 | Project restructure | 2–3 days | Step 0 |
| 2 | Foundry VTT module (read-only) | 1–2 weeks | Step 1 |
| 3 | Hub transcript viewer UI | 1–2 weeks | Step 1 (can parallel with Step 2) |
| 4 | Integration & session testing | 1 week | Steps 2 + 3 |

**Estimated total: 4–6 weeks** part-time alongside Sylvara.

Steps 2 and 3 can be parallelized — the Foundry module and the Hub UI are independent until integration in Step 4. In practice, Code will likely alternate between them.

---

## Post-v0.1 Roadmap

| Milestone | Scope | Key Features |
|---|---|---|
| **v0.2** | Suggestion engine | BYO API keys (OpenAI/Anthropic), suggestion categories, control panel, triage actions, Foundry compendium queries for rules reminders |
| **v0.3** | Campaign memory + conflict detection | Memory population methods, Ledger structure, conflict types, interrupt thresholds, resolution flow |
| **v0.4** | Campaign Setup Wizard + note import | Wizard steps (identity, tone, table setup, lines & veils, note import), Obsidian/World Anvil/PDF/docx support |
| **v0.5** | Post-session mode | Written summary, NPC/faction deltas, unresolved threads, prep suggestions, GM feedback |
| **v0.6** | Write permissions | Journal append, actor HP delta, condition add/remove — all GM-approved, never automatic |
| **v0.7** | Player-facing features | Read-only Campaign Ledger in Foundry, section visibility controls |
| **v1.0** | Production launch (local tier) | Subscription/licensing, onboarding tutorial, sample campaign, TURN relay, production signaling server |
| **v1.1** | Starfinder 2e support | SF2e system module compendiums, expanded rules data (see `brain/Lumen/lumen.md` Planned Expansions) |
| **v1.5+** | Cloud tier | Premium subscription, cloud media server (LiveKit), GPU Whisper instances, no-GPU-required tier |

---

## Business Model Summary

Two-tier subscription:

| Tier | Audio Path | Transcription | GPU Required? | Price |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Local | P2P to GM's machine | Local Whisper | Yes (6+ GB VRAM) | Free or $3–5/mo |
| Cloud | To Lumen media server | Cloud Whisper (large-v3) | No | $12–20/mo |

Both tiers share the same Hub, suggestions, memory, Foundry integration. Players don't know which tier the GM uses.

See `brain/Lumen/lumen-business.md` for detailed cost modeling and pricing rationale.

---

## PoC Results Archive

Full PoC documentation preserved for reference:

- `benchmarks/phase1_vram.md` — Whisper VRAM/latency benchmarks across all model sizes
- `benchmarks/phase3_stability.md` — 60-minute stability test results
- `poc/` — archived PoC source code
- ADRs 001–008 in `brain/Lumen/lumen-architecture.md`

---

# Sylvara — Operational Runbook
> Created: 2026-03-13
> The stuff you need when something breaks at 11pm.

## Live URLs
- **Web app:** https://scheduler.sylvara.app [EXPLICIT]
- **API:** Internal Railway networking (not publicly routable — accessed via Next.js proxy) [EXPLICIT]
- **Railway project:** https://railway.com (Sylvara project — two services + Postgres) [INFERRED]
- **Clerk dashboard:** https://dashboard.clerk.com (production instance) [INFERRED]
- **Cloudflare dashboard:** https://dash.cloudflare.com (sylvara.app zone) [INFERRED]

## Railway Services
- **web** — Next.js frontend. Serves scheduler.sylvara.app. Handles Clerk session cookies, proxy route to API. [EXPLICIT]
- **api** — Fastify REST API. Not publicly routable. Handles JWT verification, all business logic, DB access. [EXPLICIT]
- **PostgreSQL** — Railway-managed Postgres instance. [EXPLICIT]

## Database
- **Host:** Railway-provided internal hostname [EXPLICIT]
- **Port:** Railway-assigned (check Railway dashboard for current value) [INFERRED]
- **Database name:** Check `DATABASE_URL` env var in Railway [INFERRED]
- **Connection:** Via `DATABASE_URL` env var on both api and db services [EXPLICIT]
- **No passwords in this file** — check Railway dashboard or .env files on local machine

## Clerk Dashboard Key Settings
- **Production instance:** Separate from dev — uses `pk_live_*` / `sk_live_*` keys [EXPLICIT]
- **Google OAuth:** Production credentials (not reusing dev client) [EXPLICIT]
- **Domain:** 5 CNAME records required for Clerk production SSL [EXPLICIT]
- **Webhooks:** Configure endpoint URL → `https://scheduler.sylvara.app/api/webhooks/clerk` [INFERRED]
- **Webhook events:** `user.created` (at minimum) [EXPLICIT]
- **Webhook secret:** Set as `CLERK_WEBHOOK_SECRET` env var on both web and api services [EXPLICIT]
- **Domain restriction:** Configured in Clerk dashboard to limit sign-ups [EXPLICIT]
- **Branding/logo:** Requires paid Clerk tier — not available on free plan [EXPLICIT]

## Cloudflare DNS Records
- 5x CNAME records for Clerk production domain verification [EXPLICIT]
- 1x CNAME: `scheduler` → Railway internal hostname (via one-click Cloudflare Connect) [EXPLICIT]
- Root `sylvara.app` — reserved for future marketing site, no record yet [EXPLICIT]

## How To: Promote a User to MANAGER
1. User signs in at scheduler.sylvara.app (triggers Clerk `user.created` webhook → creates DB record as VIEWER) [EXPLICIT]
2. Existing MANAGER goes to Admin page in the app [EXPLICIT]
3. Find the user in the list, change role to MANAGER [EXPLICIT]
4. Role change syncs to Clerk publicMetadata automatically [EXPLICIT]
5. User's next page load reflects new role (nav shows Admin tab, mutation permissions granted) [INFERRED]

## How To: Check Railway Logs for Auth Failures
- Railway dashboard → select api service → Logs tab [INFERRED]
- Search for `[webhook-clerk]` for webhook processing issues [EXPLICIT]
- Search for `[clerk-fallback]` for publicMetadata repair failures [EXPLICIT]
- Search for `[proxy]` in web service logs for upstream fetch failures [EXPLICIT]
- A 401 from the API means: missing/invalid Bearer token, or user not found in DB [INFERRED]

## Common Failure Modes & Fixes

### Clerk ID Mismatch (dev vs production)
- **Symptom:** User authenticates but API returns 401 — "user not found" [EXPLICIT]
- **Cause:** DB user record has dev Clerk ID, but production Clerk assigns different IDs [EXPLICIT]
- **Fix:** Update `clerk_id` column in `user` table via pgAdmin or SQL: `UPDATE "User" SET "clerkId" = 'user_PROD_ID' WHERE email = 'user@example.com';` [EXPLICIT]

### CORS_ALLOWED_ORIGINS Missing https:// Prefix
- **Symptom:** CORS errors in browser console (but proxy route still works since it's server-side) [EXPLICIT]
- **Cause:** Railway env var set to `scheduler.sylvara.app` without protocol [EXPLICIT]
- **Fix:** Set to `https://scheduler.sylvara.app` (include `https://`) [EXPLICIT]

### publicMetadata Stale After Role Change
- **Symptom:** User's JWT still carries old role; frontend shows wrong permissions [EXPLICIT]
- **Cause:** Clerk publicMetadata not updated, or cached JWT not yet expired [INFERRED]
- **Fix:** Self-heals on next API request via fallback resolver. If urgent: update metadata manually in Clerk dashboard, or wait for JWT to expire (~60s). [EXPLICIT]

### Clerk Test Webhook Events Always 400
- **Symptom:** Clerk dashboard "Send test webhook" returns 400 [EXPLICIT]
- **Cause:** Test events are signed with a different key than the real `CLERK_WEBHOOK_SECRET` [EXPLICIT]
- **Fix:** This is expected behavior, not a bug. Test with a real sign-in instead. [EXPLICIT]

### NEXT_PUBLIC_ Env Var Not Taking Effect
- **Symptom:** Frontend still uses old Clerk publishable key after env var change [EXPLICIT]
- **Cause:** `NEXT_PUBLIC_*` vars are baked into the client bundle at build time [EXPLICIT]
- **Fix:** Trigger a full rebuild on Railway (redeploy the web service) [EXPLICIT]

## Deploy Process
1. Push to `main` branch on GitHub [EXPLICIT]
2. Railway auto-deploys both `web` and `api` services [EXPLICIT]
3. Migrations: if Prisma schema changed, Railway runs `prisma migrate deploy` as part of build [INFERRED]
4. Build time: ~2-3 minutes per service [ASSUMED]
5. Zero-downtime: Railway handles rolling deploys [ASSUMED]

title: Sylvara — Architecture and Technical Decisions
content:
# Sylvara — Architecture & Technical Decisions
> Created: 2026-03-13

## Infrastructure

### Railway
- Two services: `web` (Next.js) and `api` (Fastify) [EXPLICIT]
- PostgreSQL database hosted on Railway [EXPLICIT]
- Railway custom domain uses internal hostname (e.g., ttdhturu.up.railway.app), not the public URL [EXPLICIT]
- One-click Cloudflare Connect handles DNS→Railway routing automatically [EXPLICIT]
- Deploy: push to main → Railway auto-deploys both services [EXPLICIT]

### Cloudflare
- Domain `sylvara.app` registered on Cloudflare [EXPLICIT]
- DNS records: 5 Clerk CNAMEs + 1 Railway CNAME for scheduler.sylvara.app [EXPLICIT]
- SSL handled by Cloudflare proxy [INFERRED]

### PostgreSQL
- Hosted on Railway (not localhost in production) [EXPLICIT]
- All tables use UUID primary keys [EXPLICIT]
- Soft deletes (`deleted_at`) on every table [EXPLICIT]
- Prisma ORM with migrations (never edit committed migrations) [EXPLICIT]
- `numeric` types for money/hours — never use JS `number` for arithmetic [EXPLICIT]

## Auth Architecture

### Clerk Production
- Separate production instance cloned from dev [EXPLICIT]
- Production keys: `pk_live_*` / `sk_live_*` (vs `pk_test_*` / `sk_test_*` in dev) [EXPLICIT]
- User IDs are instance-specific — production IDs differ from dev [INFERRED]
- Domain restriction configured in Clerk dashboard [EXPLICIT]
- Clerk Branding (logo upload) requires paid tier — not available on free plan [EXPLICIT]

### Auth Flow
1. User signs in via Google OAuth on Clerk-hosted pages [EXPLICIT]
2. `user.created` webhook fires → Next.js webhook handler → upserts DB user + sets publicMetadata [EXPLICIT]
3. On authenticated requests: ClerkProvider (client) → clerkMiddleware (Next.js) → auth() in proxy route → getToken() → Bearer token forwarded to Fastify [EXPLICIT]
4. Fastify global preHandler: verifyToken from @clerk/backend → reads publicMetadata (userId, role) → sets request.actor [EXPLICIT]
5. Fallback: if publicMetadata missing, resolves user by clerkId from DB + fire-and-forget repair of stale metadata [EXPLICIT]

### Webhook Flow
- Clerk → POST /api/webhooks/clerk (on both Next.js and Fastify sides) [EXPLICIT]
- Svix signature verification before any processing [EXPLICIT]
- Fastify handler has a known bug: re-serializes parsed body instead of using raw bytes [EXPLICIT]
- Next.js handler correctly uses `request.text()` for raw body [EXPLICIT]
- Webhook is idempotent: findUnique by email, upsert pattern [EXPLICIT]

### Roles
- Three roles: MANAGER, SCHEDULER, VIEWER [EXPLICIT]
- New users default to VIEWER [EXPLICIT]
- MANAGER can promote/demote via Admin page [EXPLICIT]
- MANAGER/SCHEDULER can mutate; VIEWER is read-only [EXPLICIT]
- All authenticated users can read all data (by design for small team) [EXPLICIT]

## API / Proxy Architecture

### Next.js Proxy Pattern
- Frontend calls `/api/proxy?path=/api/...` [EXPLICIT]
- Proxy route: validates Clerk session → gets JWT token → forwards to Fastify API with Bearer header [EXPLICIT]
- Why: Next.js handles Clerk session cookies natively; Fastify only needs to verify the JWT [INFERRED]
- In production: `API_URL` env var points to Railway internal networking URL [EXPLICIT]
- In dev: defaults to `http://127.0.0.1:4000` [EXPLICIT]

### Fastify API
- Global auth preHandler on all `/api/*` routes (except `/api/health` and `/api/webhooks/*`) [EXPLICIT]
- Error responses: consistent shape `{ error: { code, message, details? } }` [EXPLICIT]
- Global error handler sanitizes 5xx responses — no stack traces leak [EXPLICIT]
- CORS configured via `CORS_ALLOWED_ORIGINS` env var (must include `https://` prefix) [EXPLICIT]

## Key ADRs (2026-03-13)

### ADR: DNS — scheduler.sylvara.app
- **Decision:** App lives at `scheduler.sylvara.app`; root `sylvara.app` reserved for future marketing site [EXPLICIT]
- **Context:** Needed a clean production URL. Root domain may host a marketing/landing page later.

### ADR: Clerk — Production Instance Cloned from Dev
- **Decision:** Clone dev instance to production rather than creating fresh [EXPLICIT]
- **Context:** Preserves Google OAuth configuration and social login setup. Saves reconfiguration time.

### ADR: Google OAuth — New Production Credentials
- **Decision:** Create new Google OAuth credentials for production (Option B), not reuse dev client [EXPLICIT]
- **Context:** Dev credentials were tied to localhost redirect URIs. Cleaner to create dedicated production credentials with scheduler.sylvara.app redirect URIs.

### ADR: In-App Role Management
- **Decision:** Admin page replaces CLI `promote-manager` script [EXPLICIT]
- **Context:** Non-technical users (John, Eric) need to manage roles. CLI script was dev-only tooling.
- **Implementation:** PATCH `/api/admin/users/:id/role` and `/active` endpoints, gated by requireManagerPermission. Self-modification prevented.

### ADR: Self-Healing publicMetadata
- **Decision:** JWT auth fallback resolver repairs stale Clerk publicMetadata via fire-and-forget API call [EXPLICIT]
- **Context:** When webhook hasn't run or metadata is stale (e.g., after role change), the fallback resolver in jwt-auth.ts finds the user by clerkId in DB, returns correct role, and asynchronously updates Clerk metadata so future JWTs are correct.
- **File:** `apps/api/src/server.ts` — resolveByClerkId function

- **Web app:** Next.js 15 (App Router) + React + TypeScript [EXPLICIT]
- **Styling:** Tailwind CSS (no paid UI libs; shadcn/ui allowed) [EXPLICIT]
- **API server:** Fastify + TypeScript in `apps/api` [EXPLICIT]
- **Database:** PostgreSQL (Railway-hosted in prod) [EXPLICIT]
- **ORM:** Prisma [EXPLICIT]

- **Testing:** Vitest (unit/integration) + Playwright (E2E) [EXPLICIT]

- **Primary keys:** UUID on all tables — locked, non-negotiable [EXPLICIT]

## Deployment & Infrastructure
- **Web:** Railway (Next.js service) → scheduler.sylvara.app [EXPLICIT]
- **API:** Railway (Fastify service) → internal Railway networking [EXPLICIT]
- **Database:** Railway PostgreSQL [EXPLICIT]

## Auth & Users
- Three roles: MANAGER, SCHEDULER, VIEWER [EXPLICIT]
- New users created via Clerk webhook → default to VIEWER [EXPLICIT]
- Role promotion done in-app via Admin page (MANAGER only) [EXPLICIT]
- JWT publicMetadata carries userId + role; self-heals if stale [EXPLICIT]
- Google OAuth is the only sign-in method [INFERRED]
- Domain restriction configured in Clerk dashboard [EXPLICIT]

- `orgSettings.findFirst()` missing `deletedAt: null` in 9 locations — soft-delete bypass [EXPLICIT]

# brain/lumen-business.md — Business Model & Go-to-Market
> Last updated: 2026-03-10

## Business Model

### Two-Tier Subscription

| | Tier 1 — Local | Tier 2 — Cloud |
|---|---|---|
| Audio path | P2P WebRTC → GM's machine | WebRTC → Lumen media server |
| Transcription | Local `faster-whisper` on GM's GPU | Cloud Whisper (`large-v3`) on Lumen GPU instances |
| GPU required? | Yes (6+ GB NVIDIA VRAM) | No |
| Privacy | Audio never leaves GM's machine | Audio processed in memory, never stored |
| Target user | Power users, privacy-conscious, beefy rigs | Laptops, Mac, AMD, wants best accuracy |
| Price | Free or $3–5/month | $12–20/month |
| Server cost to Lumen | ~$10–20/mo flat (signaling + TURN) | $6–20/mo per GM (GPU compute) + base infra |

Both tiers share: Hub UI, suggestion engine, memory, conflict detection, Foundry integration, Campaign Ledger, post-session mode, VoIP client. Players don't know which tier the GM uses. [EXPLICIT]

### Revenue Logic

- Local tier is a **funnel** — gets GMs using the product at low/no cost [EXPLICIT]
- Cloud tier is the **revenue driver** — removes hardware barrier, justifies premium pricing [EXPLICIT]
- BYO API keys for suggestions means Lumen doesn't absorb LLM inference costs [EXPLICIT]
- Foundry module is free — lowers adoption barrier [EXPLICIT]

### Key Pricing Constraint

Cloud tier subscription must cover GPU compute per subscriber with margin. If average compute cost per GM approaches subscription price, the model is unsustainable at small scale. [EXPLICIT]

## Cost Modeling (SPECULATIVE — Replace with PoC Data)

These estimates are pre-PoC. Phase 1 benchmarks + a post-PoC cloud cost test will produce real numbers.

| Variable | Estimate | Confidence | Source |
|---|---|---|---|
| Avg session length | 3–4 hours | Medium | Typical PF2e session length [ASSUMED] |
| Sessions per GM per month | 4–5 | Medium | Weekly game night [ASSUMED] |
| GPU compute per session-hour | $0.50–1.00 | Low | Cloud GPU pricing estimates [INFERRED] |
| Compute cost per GM per month | $6–20 | Low | Derived from above [INFERRED] |
| Media server + signaling base cost | $20–50/month | Medium | Small VPS + coturn [INFERRED] |
| Break-even subscribers (cloud tier at $15/mo) | 5–15 to cover base infra | Low | Depends on compute cost [INFERRED] |

### Break-Even Scenarios

| Compute/GM/month | Subscription | Margin/GM | Break-even on base infra ($30/mo) |
|---|---|---|---|
| $6 | $15 | $9 | ~4 subscribers |
| $10 | $15 | $5 | ~6 subscribers |
| $15 | $15 | $0 | ❌ Underwater |
| $6 | $20 | $14 | ~3 subscribers |
| $10 | $20 | $10 | ~3 subscribers |
| $15 | $20 | $5 | ~6 subscribers |

**Action required:** Post-PoC, deploy Whisper on a cloud GPU (RunPod or Modal), process a 3-hour recorded session, and measure actual compute cost. This replaces all estimates above. [EXPLICIT]

## Market

### Target User
- PF2e Game Masters running Foundry VTT v13 [EXPLICIT]
- Technically comfortable enough to install Foundry and manage a VTT [INFERRED]
- Willing to pay for GM tooling (many already pay for Foundry license, VPS hosting, content packs) [INFERRED]
- PF2e community is fiercely loyal to tools built specifically for PF2e vs. system-agnostic [INFERRED]

### Market Size (Unknown — Needs Research)
- Foundry VTT user base: unknown exact numbers. Active subreddit, active Discord [ASSUMED]
- PF2e is the second most popular TTRPG system after D&D 5e [ASSUMED]
- PF2e + Foundry overlap is a dedicated niche but not huge — this is a focused product, not a mass-market app [INFERRED]

### Competitive Landscape
- **D&D Beyond** — owned by WotC, D&D 5e only, no VTT audio integration, no real-time transcription [EXPLICIT]
- **Foundry VTT modules** — various community modules for journaling, session notes, etc. None do real-time transcription or AI suggestions [INFERRED]
- **Generic transcription tools** (Otter.ai, etc.) — not TTRPG-aware, no speaker attribution for voice chat, no Foundry integration [INFERRED]
- **No direct competitor** doing real-time transcription + AI GM assistance + Foundry integration for PF2e [INFERRED]

### Differentiation
- VoIP-first with deterministic speaker attribution (no one else does this for TTRPGs) [EXPLICIT]
- PF2e-native — uses PF2e rules compendium, not generic [EXPLICIT]
- Foundry VTT integration (direct journal writes, actor updates) [EXPLICIT]
- Campaign memory with conflict detection [EXPLICIT]
- Local-first option with strong privacy story [EXPLICIT]

## Go-to-Market (Speculative — Post-PoC)

### Channels
- PF2e Foundry Discord community [INFERRED]
- r/Pathfinder2e and r/FoundryVTT subreddits [INFERRED]
- Foundry VTT package listing (free connector module) [EXPLICIT]
- Word of mouth in actual-play and TTRPG communities [ASSUMED]

### Launch Strategy Options (Decide Post-PoC)

**(a) Local tier only** — ship fast, minimal infra, prove the product. Add cloud later.
- Pros: fastest to market, lowest cost, strongest privacy story
- Cons: narrow hardware audience, no premium revenue at launch

**(b) Both tiers simultaneously** — more engineering but wider market at launch.
- Pros: captures both power users and laptop GMs immediately
- Cons: more infrastructure to build and maintain, higher launch cost

**(c) Cloud-first** — if local tier proves too fragile across diverse GPU hardware.
- Pros: simplest user experience (no GPU concerns)
- Cons: weakest privacy story, highest infrastructure cost, no free tier

Cole's current leaning: local tier first, cloud tier later [INFERRED from "ship local, prove the product" discussion]

### Paizo Relationship
- Cole initially considered selling Lumen to Paizo [EXPLICIT]
- This was pushed back on — Paizo is a publisher, not a software company. They don't acquire desktop apps with real-time audio processing [EXPLICIT]
- The right model is a standalone subscription product serving the PF2e community directly [EXPLICIT]
- Paizo partnership (endorsement, licensing, API access) could be valuable later but is not required [INFERRED]

## Open Business Questions

- [ ] What's the actual addressable market size? (PF2e GMs on Foundry with compatible hardware)
- [ ] Price sensitivity: would GMs pay $15/mo? $20/mo? (Post-PoC community survey)
- [ ] What hardware do PF2e Foundry GMs actually have? (Survey to validate spec tiers)
- [ ] Cloud compute cost per session — is the margin viable? (Post-PoC benchmark)
- [ ] Freemium vs. flat local tier pricing? (Free local builds the funnel faster but generates no revenue)

---
*Update cost estimates with real data after PoC benchmarking. Tag confidence levels on all facts.*
